D3 Chemistry L3 Apply Reference

Wet Denim — Index Matching Hides the Air Pockets

dry fabric is bright because air pockets (n=1) scatter light at every fibre; water (n=1.33) closes the index gap and the fabric goes dark — same fabric, different optics

A dry textile is essentially fibre (n≈1.5) embedded in air (n=1). Every fibre surface scatters because of the huge Δn — and that diffuse scatter is what makes dry fabric look pale. Soak the fibre and water (n=1.33) replaces the air; Δn drops from 0.5 to 0.17; scattering drops; the underlying dye colour shows through and the fabric looks darker and more saturated.

Drag the wetness fraction. The fabric swatch darkens; the air-vs-water scattering bar shrinks; the simulated cross-section shows water filling the pores.
